title.en: Chit Fund or Committee (BC) Default? Recovering Your Money from Foremen and Members

answerBox.en: Registered chit funds are governed by the Chit Funds Act, 1982 — disputes go to the Registrar of Chits, whose arbitration awards are enforceable as decrees, and the foreman's security deposit with the Registrar backs your claim. Unregistered "committees" (BCs) are contract + criminal territory: acknowledgements, legal notice, civil suit — and cheating/criminal-breach-of-trust FIRs where the organiser absconds. The BUDS Act 2019 adds attachment of assets for unregulated deposit schemes.

en.label: FIR (absconding organiser)
en.startsFrom: assets move quickly — so must you

en.title: Establish what the scheme legally is
en.summary: Registered chit, unregistered committee, or a deposit scam.
en.detail: Check whether the chit is **registered** (chit agreement, registration number, Registrar of Chits of the state — ask or verify at the registrar's office). Registered chits give you the Act's machinery and the **foreman's 100% security deposit** held with the Registrar. An informal **committee/BC** is a mutual contract among members — enforceable, but without the statutory net. A scheme promising fixed "returns" to non-participants may be an **unregulated deposit scheme** — squarely inside the [BUDS Act, 2019](https://www.indiacode.nic.in) with its attachment machinery.

en.title: Assemble payments and demand
en.summary: Passbook, receipts, transfers, the member list — then notice.
en.detail: Collect your chit passbook/receipts, bank/UPI records of contributions, the member list and the agreement (or chat records for informal BCs — the group chat usually documents everything). Send the foreman/organiser a **written demand**; for prized-but-unpaid or wound-up chits, follow with the **₹999 advocate's notice** to the foreman and sureties. Members who received the pot and stopped contributing get the same treatment — their liability to the fund is several and provable.

en.title: Registered chits: the Registrar's arbitration
en.summary: Cheap, purpose-built, decree-force awards against the deposit.
en.detail: Under **Sections 64–71, Chit Funds Act**, disputes between subscribers and foremen go to the **Registrar (or nominee) as arbitrator** — filing fees are small, procedure summary, and the **award executes as a civil decree**, with the foreman's security deposit available. Complain in parallel to the Registrar's supervisory side: registrars can inspect, freeze fresh chit business, and wind up defaulting foremen — pressure that produces settlements.

en.title: Fraud/absconding: criminal + BUDS attachment
en.summary: FIR fast; BUDS competent authority attaches assets.
en.detail: Organiser vanished or diverting funds? File the **FIR immediately** — cheating **Section 420 IPC (now Section 318 BNS)** and criminal breach of trust **Section 406 IPC (now Section 316 BNS)**; group FIRs by multiple members get economic-offences-wing attention. For unregulated deposit schemes, the **BUDS Act** empowers the state's competent authority to **attach the scheme's and promoters' assets** and repay depositors through designated courts — ask the district collector's office who the notified competent authority is, and get every victim to file. Speed decides recovery: attachable assets evaporate.

rows.label: Consultation (scheme classification)
rows.note: Which regime applies decides the route

en.footnote: Joining next time: prefer registered chits (verify at the Registrar), never hand cash without receipts, and treat schemes promising assured returns for "just depositing" as presumptively illegal under BUDS.

en: committee wale paise nahi de rahe

en.q: Our committee was informal — no registration, just a group. Is my money legally recoverable?
en.a: Yes — an informal BC is a contract among members: your contribution record and the group's chats/records support a civil claim against the organiser (and pot-takers who stopped paying). It lacks the Chit Funds Act machinery, so the route is notice → suit/Lok Adalat, plus FIR where dishonesty is evident.

en.q: What does the foreman's security deposit mean for me?
en.a: A registered chit's foreman deposits 100% of the chit value with the Registrar before starting. On default, arbitration awards can be satisfied from it — which is why the registered-chit route has meaningfully better recovery odds than informal committees.

en.q: The organiser is paying some members and stalling others. Does that matter legally?
en.a: Selective payment strengthens the dishonesty inference for the criminal complaint and shows funds exist — cite it in the FIR and the Registrar complaint. It also justifies urgency: interim attachment (civil or BUDS) before remaining funds are distributed to favourites.

en.q: Is a "double your money" or fixed-return scheme the same as a chit?
en.a: No — a chit rotates the members' own pot by auction/lot; a scheme taking deposits against promised returns is deposit-taking, which unregistered entities cannot lawfully do. BUDS Act 2019 criminalises such schemes and provides the attachment-and-repayment machinery; report to police and the competent authority.

en.q: Many of us are affected. Do we file together or separately?
en.a: Both: one joint/group FIR (or multiple FIRs consolidated) for the criminal side — numbers move EOW/police priority — and individual claims (arbitration/suit) for your specific amounts. Coordinated victims also file the BUDS application together, which accelerates the attachment decision.

reviewer: Adv. Rohan Mehta, Bar Council of Maharashtra & Goa, MAH/2214/2011
author: WakilBhai Editorial Team
